seoTitle: How to Use Snapshots to Track Data History in Power BI
metaDescription: Track how your data changes over time in Power BI using snapshots, so you can compare past and current figures for better reporting.
intro: Sometimes a single point-in-time report isn't enough, and you need to see how figures like sales or inventory have changed over time. This guide covers how to use the snapshot approach in Power BI to capture and compare historical data. It's useful for teams that need trend visibility for compliance, forecasting, or performance tracking.

steps.heading: Set up snapshots in Power BI
steps.body: Configure your report or data pipeline to periodically capture and store a snapshot of your data at set intervals.

steps.heading: View historical data
steps.body: Access the stored snapshots within your report so you can see what your data looked like at different points in time.

steps.heading: Compare data over time
steps.body: Build visuals that place snapshots side by side, letting you compare metrics like quarterly sales or inventory levels across periods.

steps.heading: Automate ongoing reporting with snapshots
steps.body: Set up your snapshot process to run automatically so historical comparisons stay current without manual data capture.

steps.heading: Apply it to a business scenario
steps.body: Use snapshots for a practical case, such as tracking changes in inventory levels or comparing quarterly sales performance.

faq.question: What is a snapshot in Power BI used for?
faq.answer: A snapshot captures your data at a specific point in time so you can later compare it against current or future data.

faq.question: Do snapshots require extra storage?
faq.answer: Yes, since each snapshot stores a copy of your data at that point in time, so storage needs grow as you keep more historical captures.

faq.question: Can I automate how often snapshots are taken?
faq.answer: Yes, snapshot capture can be automated to run on a schedule so historical tracking stays consistent without manual effort.

faq.question: Why would a business need to track data history?
faq.answer: Tracking history supports use cases like compliance, trend analysis, and comparing performance across different time periods.

faq.question: Is the snapshot approach only useful for large enterprises?
faq.answer: No, any team that needs to compare data over time, regardless of size, can benefit from tracking snapshots.
